h1Title: ADHD: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental condition characterised by persistent, developmentally inappropriate levels of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity originating before age 12. Approximately 5-7% of children and 2.5-4% of adults worldwide meet diagnostic criteria, making it one of the most prevalent psychiatric conditions across the lifespan. The disorder spans three presentations — predominantly inattentive, predominantly hyperactive-impulsive, and combined — each producing distinct functional impairments at school, work, and in relationships. Without adequate treatment, ADHD is associated with academic underachievement, occupational difficulties, relationship breakdown, and a roughly two- to three-fold increased risk of accidental injury. Evidence-based treatment combining stimulant medication with behavioural strategies achieves meaningful symptom reduction in 70-80% of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: 5-7% of children and 2.5-4% of adults worldwide (Polanczyk et al. 2015, Simon et al. 2009)
keyStats.demographics: Boys diagnosed 2-3× more often than girls in childhood; adult sex ratio approaches 1:1 as hyperactive symptoms diminish and inattentive presentation becomes more prominent
keyStats.avgAge: Symptoms must be present before age 12; median diagnosis age approximately 7 years in children, though adult diagnosis peaks in early adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 366 million adults worldwide live with ADHD (Fayyad et al. 2017, World Federation for ADHD)

definition: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ICD-10: F90; DSM-5: 314) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by a persistent pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity that interferes with functioning or development. Neurobiologically, ADHD involves dysregulation of catecholaminergic (dopamine and noradrenaline) signalling in prefrontal-striatal circuits governing executive function, working memory, response inhibition, and emotional regulation. Brain imaging studies document an average 3-5 year delay in cortical maturation in affected children and smaller caudate nucleus and prefrontal cortex volumes. DSM-5 requires at least six symptoms of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity in children (five in adults ≥17), present in two or more settings, causing clinically meaningful impairment, onset before age 12, and not better explained by another condition. Three presentations are specified: predominantly inattentive (ADHD-PI), predominantly hyperactive-impulsive (ADHD-PHI), and combined (ADHD-C). ADHD-C is the most common, accounting for roughly 55-60% of clinical presentations. The condition is managed principally by psychiatrists, paediatric neurologists, and developmental paediatricians, with psychologists and specialised nurse practitioners providing behavioural and psychoeducational components.

typesClassification.description: Characterised by at least six inattentive symptoms (careless errors, difficulty sustaining attention, not listening, incomplete tasks, organisational failure, avoidance of effortful tasks, losing items, distractibility, forgetfulness) without prominent hyperactivity. More common in girls and adults; often missed because it lacks the disruptive behaviour of the hyperactive type.

At least six hyperactivity-impulsivity symptoms (fidgeting, leaving seat, running/climbing, inability to play quietly, on-the-go, excessive talking, blurting answers, difficulty waiting turn, interrupting others) without prominent inattention. More common in young boys; often transitions to combined type as children mature.

Meets criteria for both inattentive and hyperactive-impulsive symptom clusters. The most prevalent clinical presentation, affecting approximately 55-60% of those diagnosed; associated with more pervasive functional impairment than either pure subtype.

Up to 65% of children retain clinically significant symptoms into adulthood, though hyperactivity often diminishes while inattention and executive dysfunction persist. Adults present predominantly with difficulties at work, chronic disorganisation, relationship instability, and emotional dysregulation rather than overt motor restlessness.

primarySymptoms: Persistent difficulty sustaining attention on tasks or play activities, resulting in frequent careless mistakes and unfinished projects at school, work, or home.
Appearing not to listen when spoken to directly, often described by others as 'in their own world', even in the absence of obvious distraction.
Chronic organisational failure — missed deadlines, lost materials (keys, phone, homework, paperwork), and inability to sequence multi-step tasks.
Marked avoidance of tasks requiring sustained mental effort, such as homework, reports, or form completion, with clear distress when avoidance is not possible.
Excessive fidgeting, squirming, tapping hands or feet, or inability to remain seated when sitting is expected — the hallmark of the hyperactive presentation.
Impulsive interrupting of conversations or games, blurting out answers before questions are completed, and difficulty waiting for a turn.
Emotional dysregulation — low frustration tolerance, quick irritability, sudden emotional reactions disproportionate to the trigger, and rapid mood shifts.
Difficulty regulating sleep-wake cycles, with many individuals struggling to wind down at night despite daytime fatigue.

Hyperfocusing on preferred, highly stimulating activities for hours (gaming, art, sports) while unable to sustain attention on less engaging tasks — a paradoxical pattern reflecting dysregulated attentional control.
Frequent forgetfulness in daily activities — missing appointments, forgetting to return calls, failing to follow through on routine chores.
earlyWarningSigns: Preschool-age child who cannot remain engaged in structured activities for age-appropriate periods, is persistently impulsive, and is expelled from or causes significant disruption at nursery.
School-age child with discrepancy between apparent intelligence and academic output, whose teacher repeatedly notes inattention, rushing, or class disruption across multiple school years.
Adolescent with escalating academic failure, multiple incomplete assignments, and rising anxiety around study tasks despite normal cognitive testing.

Adult newly experiencing chronic job difficulties, relationship strain, or financial disorganisation after a relatively high-structure environment (school, military) no longer compensates for executive weaknesses.
emergencySigns: Active suicidal ideation or self-harm in a young person with ADHD — comorbid depression and impulsivity markedly raise suicide risk and require urgent psychiatric review.
Acute psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, severe disorganisation) emerging in a patient recently started on stimulant medication — discontinue immediately and seek same-day assessment.
Cardiovascular symptoms (chest pain, palpitations, syncope) in a patient taking a stimulant or atomoxetine — warrants urgent ECG and cardiological evaluation.
Stimulant misuse or diversion with signs of intoxication or withdrawal in a young person — address safety, reduce access, and reassess the prescribing plan.

causes.description: ADHD has a heritability of approximately 74-76% from twin studies (Faraone et al. 2021). Multiple common genetic variants of small effect, as well as rare copy-number variants (e.g. deletions at 16p13, 22q11), are implicated. First-degree relatives of an individual with ADHD carry a 2-8 times greater risk of meeting criteria themselves.

Reduced dopaminergic and noradrenergic tone in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and striatum impairs executive function, working memory, and inhibitory control. This is the neurobiological mechanism targeted by first-line stimulant medications.

causes.cause: Prenatal exposure to nicotine, alcohol, or psychoactive substances
causes.description: Maternal smoking during pregnancy increases child ADHD risk approximately 2.3-fold (Motlagh et al. 2011). Foetal alcohol spectrum disorder overlaps substantially with ADHD symptomatically. Prenatal opioid and cocaine exposure are also associated with attention deficits, though genetic confounding is difficult to separate.

causes.cause: Extremely premature birth or very low birth weight
causes.description: Children born before 28 weeks gestation have a 2.6-fold higher rate of ADHD diagnosis, likely reflecting disrupted prefrontal white-matter development and periventricular leukomalacia affecting corticostriatal tracts.

Blood lead concentration above 5 µg/dL in early childhood is associated with a 2-fold increase in ADHD diagnosis. Prenatal organophosphate pesticide exposure at detectable urinary levels is linked to a roughly 1.5-2-fold increase in inattentive symptoms in several prospective cohorts.

causes.cause: Psychosocial adversity (risk moderator, not primary cause)
causes.description: Institutional deprivation, severe neglect, or exposure to early trauma can produce ADHD-like behaviours. Adversity appears to moderate genetic risk rather than independently cause ADHD, but distinguishing reactive-attachment disinhibition from ADHD in children with adverse histories requires careful clinical evaluation.

riskFactors.description: A parent with ADHD confers approximately 40-57% risk in offspring; sibling concordance 25-35%. The strongest single risk factor for the condition.

Boys are diagnosed 2-3× more frequently than girls before puberty, partly due to higher rates of hyperactive-impulsive presentation that triggers clinical referral. Adult sex ratios are closer to parity.
Maternal smoking during pregnancy approximately doubles child ADHD risk independently of socioeconomic confounders in meta-analyses of over 500,000 participants.
Prematurity increases ADHD prevalence 2-4-fold; the risk increases stepwise with decreasing gestational age.
Very low birth weight is an independent predictor of ADHD, executive dysfunction, and learning difficulties, reflecting disrupted early brain development.
Blood lead levels above 5 µg/dL — still encountered in homes with lead paint or plumbing — are associated with a 2-fold increase in ADHD incidence; effect is dose-dependent.
Approximately 40-50% of children with ADHD have a comorbid learning disorder and 25-35% have an anxiety disorder, each worsening overall functional outcome and complicating diagnosis.

affectedDemographics: Children and adolescents: peak diagnostic prevalence at ages 6-12 years; ADHD is the most commonly diagnosed neurodevelopmental disorder in this age group in high-income countries.
Adults: an estimated 2.5-4% of adults worldwide meet criteria; a large proportion were never diagnosed in childhood, especially women with inattentive presentations.
Girls and women: historically under-diagnosed because the inattentive presentation is more common in females; girls are referred later and receive diagnosis on average 3-5 years after boys.
Lower socioeconomic groups: ADHD prevalence is elevated in areas with higher rates of lead exposure, prenatal substance exposure, and reduced access to accurate assessment.
diagnosisOverview: ADHD diagnosis is clinical, based on a structured psychiatric or developmental assessment — there is no biomarker or imaging test that confirms the diagnosis. The clinician collects a detailed developmental and psychiatric history, obtains standardised rating scales from parents and teachers (Conners, SNAP-IV, Vanderbilt), reviews academic records and any prior psychological testing, and conducts a mental-state examination. DSM-5 requires a minimum of six inattentive and/or six hyperactive-impulsive symptoms in children (five in adults aged 17+), present for at least 6 months at a severity inconsistent with developmental level, in at least two settings, with onset before age 12, and with clear evidence of functional impairment. The key differential diagnoses are anxiety disorder (cognitive impairment from worry rather than executive dysfunction), depression (secondary attentional difficulties), sleep deprivation or sleep-disordered breathing, learning disorder, autism spectrum disorder, and substance use. Thyroid disease and fragile-X syndrome should be considered in appropriate clinical contexts. Neuropsychological testing is not required for diagnosis but adds value when intellectual disability, learning disorders, or giftedness confound the presentation. Brain MRI and EEG are not part of routine ADHD assessment. The NICE 2018 guideline (NG87) recommends that diagnosis in children should be made by specialists (consultant paediatricians, psychiatrists, or clinical psychologists with training in neurodevelopmental conditions).

diagnosticTests.test: Standardised ADHD rating scales (Conners-3, SNAP-IV, Vanderbilt, ADHD-RS-5)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantify symptom severity and frequency across home and school/work settings; required to support clinical diagnosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Parents and teachers complete questionnaires of 18-90 items about the child's behaviour over the past 6 months. Adults self-report on validated scales (ASRS-5, Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale). Takes 10-20 minutes per rater. Scores are compared to age- and sex-specific normative data.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, maps onset, duration, and cross-situational impairment, and screens for comorbidities
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 45-90 minute interview with the patient and, for children, a caregiver. The clinician asks about specific symptoms, their duration, onset age, and impact on school, work, and relationships.

diagnosticTests.test: Full-scale IQ and neuropsychological testing (WISC-V, WAIS-IV, BRIEF-2)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies cognitive profile, identifies learning disorders, and characterises executive function deficits; most valuable when diagnosis is uncertain or co-morbid learning disability is suspected
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 2-4 hour battery of standardised tasks administered by a psychologist. Involves puzzles, memory exercises, and timed activities. Results returned within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Physical and neurological examination with blood tests (TSH, FBC, ferritin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes thyroid dysfunction, iron-deficiency anaemia, and other medical conditions that mimic ADHD before prescribing stimulants
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard physical examination and routine blood draw. Results within 1-2 days for most panels.

diagnosticTests.test: ECG before stimulant prescription in selected patients
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for long QT syndrome, arrhythmia, or structural heart disease in patients with cardiac risk factors or family history of sudden cardiac death
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 10-lead ECG taking 5 minutes; result immediately available.

diagnosticTests.test: Review of academic/occupational records and collateral information
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents multi-setting impairment, establishes developmental onset, and provides an objective longitudinal picture of function
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: School reports, old teacher notes, or employer assessments reviewed by the clinician. Not a formal test; gathered as part of the diagnostic workup.

treatmentOverview: ADHD treatment is guided by the NICE NG87 (2018), AAP 2019, and CADDRA 2020 guidelines, all of which recommend a multimodal approach with psychoeducation as the first step for all ages. For children aged 5 and under, behaviour therapy and parent training are first-line before medication. For school-age children and adults, stimulant medication plus behavioural intervention produces the best outcomes. Methylphenidate and amphetamine-based stimulants (lisdexamfetamine, dextroamphetamine, mixed amphetamine salts) are first-line pharmacotherapy; they increase synaptic dopamine and noradrenaline in the prefrontal cortex and normalise striatal-cortical signalling. Response rates of 70-80% are consistently reported in randomised trials, with effect sizes (Cohen's d) of 0.8-1.1 for stimulant medication on symptom rating scales. When stimulants are contraindicated (cardiac history, severe anxiety, tic disorder) or ineffective, non-stimulant options include atomoxetine (selective noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor), guanfacine extended-release, and clonidine extended-release. Cognitive-behavioural therapy adapted for ADHD, organisational skills training, mindfulness-based programmes, and neurofeedback have evidence for adjunctive benefit, particularly in adults and adolescents. School-based accommodations (extended time, reduced-distraction settings, chunked assignments) are a critical and often overlooked component of management. Dose titration under specialist supervision typically requires 4-8 weeks to identify the optimal medication, dose, and formulation for each individual. Treatment is typically long-term: most adults continue pharmacotherapy indefinitely, with medication-free trials every 1-3 years to reassess ongoing clinical need.

medicalTreatments.name: Methylphenidate (0.3-1 mg/kg/day; long-acting formulations once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line stimulant for children and adolescents per NICE NG87. Blocks dopamine and noradrenaline reuptake. Available in immediate-release (4-6 h duration) and long-acting (8-12 h) formulations. Monitor height, weight, blood pressure, and heart rate every 6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 70-80% response rate; effect size d=0.9-1.1 in meta-analyses of paediatric RCTs (Cortese et al. 2018)

medicalTreatments.name: Lisdexamfetamine (20-70 mg once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Prodrug of dextroamphetamine; slower onset reduces misuse potential. First-line stimulant in many adult guidelines. NICE-approved for adult ADHD when methylphenidate is inadequate.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate 70-75% in adults in the ADORE study; effect size d=0.8-1.0

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamfetamine / mixed amphetamine salts (5-40 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Second stimulant option; particularly useful when methylphenidate is poorly tolerated. Dose split into 2-3 doses for immediate-release, or once daily for extended-release (Adderall XR, Vyvanse variants). Common side effects: appetite suppression, insomnia, headache.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to methylphenidate; preferred in some adults with prolonged duration-of-action requirements

medicalTreatments.name: Atomoxetine (0.5-1.2 mg/kg/day in children; 40-100 mg/day in adults)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-stimulant selective noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor. Preferred when stimulants cause intolerance, abuse risk is high, or significant anxiety or tic disorder co-exists. Full effect requires 6-8 weeks of treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effect size d=0.6-0.7 versus placebo; response rate approximately 50-65%

medicalTreatments.name: Guanfacine ER (1-7 mg once daily in children; adjunct in adults)
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist. Particularly useful for emotional dysregulation, tics, and hyperactivity. Can be combined with stimulants for insufficient response to monotherapy. Causes sedation and blood-pressure reduction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effect size d=0.5-0.6 versus placebo in paediatric trials; 30-40% symptom reduction as monotherapy

medicalTreatments.name: Viloxazine ER (100-400 mg/day; approved USA only)
medicalTreatments.description: Newer non-stimulant noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor approved by FDA (2021) for paediatric ADHD. Alternative to atomoxetine with potentially faster onset (4 weeks). Not yet available in UK or Europe.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Statistically significant reduction in ADHD-RS-5 versus placebo in phase 3 trials; response rate approximately 50-60%

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) adapted for ADHD
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured 12-18 session programme targeting organisational skills, planning, emotional regulation, and impulsivity. A 2010 Safren et al. RCT (PMID 20873900) showed significant ADHD symptom and functional benefit versus relaxation in adults on stable medication.

alternativeTreatments.name: Parent management training (PMT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured coaching in differential reinforcement, planned ignoring, and contingency management. Recommended as first-line by NICE in children under 5 and as adjunct in school-age children. Effect size d=0.55-0.75 on parent-rated behaviour (Kaminski et al. meta-analysis, 2008).

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Meta-analysis by Hawkey & Nigg (2014, PMID 24882025) found a small but statistically significant benefit (effect size d=0.31) for combined EPA+DHA supplementation on ADHD total symptoms; most useful as adjunct, not replacement, for pharmacotherapy.

whySeeSpecialist: A consultant psychiatrist, paediatric neurologist, or developmental paediatrician should confirm the ADHD diagnosis before prescribing stimulants, particularly when comorbidities such as autism, bipolar disorder, psychosis, or substance use are present. Specialist input is essential for titration of combination pharmacotherapy and management of treatment-resistant cases.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a psychiatrist or paediatrician with documented expertise in neurodevelopmental conditions and at least two years' post-qualification experience managing ADHD. For adults, specialists in adult ADHD clinics are preferred; for children, developmental-behavioural paediatricians or child psychiatrists. Ensure the clinic uses standardised rating scales and has access to psychological testing.
hospitalCriteria: Complex diagnostic uncertainty with comorbid autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disability, or bipolar disorder
Severe self-harm risk or suicidality with ADHD in adolescents
Inpatient stabilisation when stimulants have produced psychotic symptoms or severe cardiovascular adverse events
Child and adolescent mental health services (CAMHS) or adult ADHD clinic with access to non-stimulant prescribers
keyFacilities: Specialist ADHD clinics within CAMHS or adult outpatient psychiatry
Educational psychology departments for school-based assessment
preventionStrategies: Avoid tobacco and alcohol use during pregnancy — prenatal smoking is the most modifiable risk factor for ADHD, approximately doubling child risk.
Reduce childhood exposure to lead by testing homes built before 1978 for lead paint, using water filters rated for lead removal, and ensuring children are tested at ages 1 and 2.
Ensure adequate prenatal care and manage conditions that increase risk of premature birth, which is independently associated with a 2-4-fold ADHD risk.
Screen all children with known risk factors (family history, premature birth, low birth weight) at school entry to enable early intervention before academic difficulties entrenched.

Provide structured, predictable environments and consistent routines in early childhood for children showing early behavioural signs — reduces functional impairment even in the absence of formal diagnosis.
lifestyleModifications: Take medication consistently at the same time each day; keep a simple routine log to detect breakthrough symptoms that suggest dose adjustment is needed.
Prioritise sleep hygiene — ADHD is associated with delayed sleep onset; melatonin 0.5-5 mg 30-60 minutes before target bedtime reduces sleep-onset latency in children and adults.
Regular aerobic exercise (30 minutes, 3-5 times per week) — meta-analyses show moderate effect sizes (d=0.44-0.56) on executive function and attention in people with ADHD.
Use external organisational tools consistently — phone reminders, visible calendars, structured task lists, and 'launch pad' areas for essential items reduce daily functional failures.
Break large tasks into timed segments (Pomodoro method: 25-minute work blocks with 5-minute breaks) to work with, not against, the ADHD attention cycle.
Limit highly stimulating screen time in the 2 hours before bed; excessive gaming and social-media use worsen sleep-onset difficulties and can exacerbate inattention.
Engage with an ADHD psychoeducation programme or peer-support group — adults who understand their neurobiological profile show better medication adherence and coping outcomes.

recommended: Regular balanced meals at consistent times — skipping meals worsens appetite suppression from stimulants and can destabilise energy and attention
Omega-3 rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) — EPA+DHA supplementation shows a small benefit on ADHD symptoms in meta-analyses
Iron-rich foods (lean red meat, lentils, fortified cereals) — low ferritin (<30 ng/mL) correlates with worse ADHD symptoms in children in several observational studies
Protein-containing breakfast to counteract stimulant-induced appetite suppression and stabilise blood glucose and alertness
avoid: High-sugar beverages and ultra-processed snacks causing blood-glucose spikes and crashes that amplify attention difficulties
Caffeine in children and adolescents on stimulant medication — increases heart rate, worsens insomnia, and provides no additional cognitive benefit
Alcohol in adults with ADHD — interacts poorly with stimulants and non-stimulants, impairs executive function, and increases impulsivity risk
Skipping evening meals on stimulant medication days — appetite suppression can cause undernutrition and growth deceleration in children

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise is a recognised adjunct for ADHD management. Guidelines recommend at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week for adults, and 60 minutes of vigorous activity daily for children. Team sports with structured rules additionally build impulse control and social skills. Exercise should be scheduled rather than spontaneous, as ADHD impairs initiation of low-stimulation activities.
dailyManagement: Take stimulant medication with or after breakfast to reduce nausea and maximise the therapeutic window through the school or work day.
Use a visual schedule posted in a prominent location to anchor the day's key tasks and transition points.
Check in with a daily planner or digital task manager at the same time each morning and evening to capture commitments and review progress.
Ensure a consistent wind-down routine from 9 pm — screen brightness reduction, stimulus reduction, and consistent bedtime reduce sleep-onset latency.
Track any side effects (appetite, sleep, mood, blood pressure) in a brief weekly log to bring to clinic reviews every 3-6 months.
Communicate ADHD status and accommodation needs to school or workplace formally — documented accommodations (extended time, quiet workspace) are a legal right in most jurisdictions.
prognosis: ADHD is a chronic neurodevelopmental condition, but outcomes vary markedly with early diagnosis, treatment adherence, and support systems. Approximately 50-65% of children retain clinically significant symptoms into adulthood requiring ongoing management. Adults with treated ADHD achieve substantially better academic completion rates, occupational stability, and relationship satisfaction compared with untreated peers. The MTA Cooperative Group's long-term follow-up (Molina et al. 2009) found that early sustained stimulant treatment predicted better adolescent outcomes compared with medication management alone. Untreated ADHD carries a roughly 1.5-2-fold elevated risk of substance use disorder, 2-3-fold risk of motor vehicle accidents, and significantly higher rates of anxiety and depression as secondary comorbidities. Life expectancy may be modestly reduced (approximately 9-13 years based on Danish registry data) in adults with severe ADHD and comorbid conditions, primarily driven by accidents and cardiovascular risks from untreated comorbidities rather than ADHD per se.
recoveryTimeline: Stimulant medication produces symptomatic benefit within the first 1-2 weeks of correct dosing. Dose optimisation typically requires 4-8 weeks of titration under specialist supervision. Behavioural and CBT-based gains accumulate over 3-6 months of consistent practice. Accommodations at school or work typically improve function within the first academic term of implementation. For adults, full benefit from multimodal treatment is usually evident at 6-12 months.
complications: Academic underachievement and school dropout — untreated ADHD children are 2-3× more likely to repeat a year or require special educational placement.

Substance use disorder — ADHD confers a roughly 2-fold elevated risk for alcohol and stimulant misuse; adolescents with untreated ADHD are at highest risk, with treatment reducing (but not eliminating) this risk.
Depression and anxiety as secondary comorbidities — 40-50% of adults with ADHD develop clinically significant depression at some point; emotional dysregulation mediates much of this risk.
Road traffic accidents — young adult drivers with ADHD have crash rates approximately 2-4× higher than non-ADHD drivers; medication markedly reduces this risk during active dosing hours.
Relationship and occupational instability — ADHD adults change jobs significantly more frequently and have higher divorce rates than matched controls in Scandinavian registry studies.
Growth deceleration in children on stimulants — height and weight velocity may reduce 1-2 cm/year during the first 1-2 years on stimulants; medication holidays and monitoring mitigate this risk.

supportResources.name: CHADD (Children and Adults with ADHD)
supportResources.description: The leading US non-profit for ADHD education, advocacy, and support groups for parents, adults, and educators.

supportResources.name: ADHD UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing resources, a helpline, and peer support for adults and families navigating ADHD diagnosis and treatment.

supportResources.name: NICE NG87 (2018): Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline covering diagnosis and management across the lifespan, freely accessible to patients and clinicians.

supportResources.name: CDC — What is ADHD?
supportResources.description: US CDC evidence-based overview of ADHD prevalence, diagnosis, and treatment used by public health programmes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anxiety Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both produce inattention and restlessness, but anxiety-driven inattention stems from intrusive worry and hyperarousal; the patient is typically preoccupied with feared outcomes rather than globally inattentive. Anxiety features avoidance of anxiety-triggering (not merely effortful) tasks, somatic symptoms of autonomic arousal, and responds to SSRI or CBT as first-line rather than stimulants.

confusedWithConditions.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ADHD and autism co-occur in approximately 50% of cases, but autism additionally involves qualitative differences in social reciprocity, restricted and repetitive behaviours, and sensory sensitivities not accounted for by inattention or hyperactivity. When both are present, both should be diagnosed and managed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bipolar Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Episodic grandiosity, markedly decreased need for sleep, pressured speech, and hypersexuality distinguish a manic or hypomanic episode from ADHD. ADHD symptoms are chronic and stable from childhood; bipolar mood episodes are episodic with clear periods of normal functioning between episodes. Stimulants can trigger mania in unrecognised bipolar disorder, making accurate diagnosis critical.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression causes secondary inattention, psychomotor slowing, and poor concentration that can mimic ADHD-PI. Depression features pervasive anhedonia, low mood, neurovegetative symptoms, and typical onset later in adolescence or adulthood, whereas ADHD onset is before age 12 and persists continuously rather than episodically.

relatedConditions.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares neurodevelopmental origins and executive function deficits; co-occurs in approximately 30-50% of ADHD cases and is managed by the same specialist teams.

relatedConditions.name: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve dysregulated frontostriatal circuits; can co-occur and complicate pharmacological management as stimulants may worsen OCD compulsions.

relatedConditions.name: Bipolar Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares emotional dysregulation and impulsivity; differentiating the two is essential before initiating stimulants, which can precipitate mania.

relatedConditions.name: Opioid Use Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Untreated ADHD is a significant risk factor for substance use disorders including opioid misuse; co-management requires careful stimulant prescribing.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of ADHD in adults?
faqs.answer: Adults with ADHD most commonly experience chronic disorganisation, difficulty completing tasks, frequent forgetfulness, emotional dysregulation, impulsive decision-making, and poor time management. The overt hyperactivity seen in children typically diminishes, but inner restlessness and difficulty sustaining effort on unengaging tasks persist.

faqs.question: How is ADHD diagnosed in children?
faqs.answer: ADHD is diagnosed clinically by a specialist using standardised rating scales completed by parents and teachers, a structured interview about symptoms in multiple settings, and exclusion of other causes. At least six inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms must be present for at least 6 months with onset before age 12 per DSM-5 criteria.

faqs.question: Is ADHD a real medical condition?
faqs.answer: Yes. ADHD is recognised by the WHO, APA, NICE, and all major medical bodies as a neurodevelopmental disorder with well-established neurobiological correlates including differences in prefrontal-striatal dopamine signalling and cortical maturation delay on brain MRI. It has a heritability of approximately 74-76% from twin studies.

faqs.question: What is the first-line treatment for ADHD?
faqs.answer: For school-age children and adults, stimulant medication (methylphenidate or amphetamine-based) combined with behavioural therapy is first-line per NICE NG87 and AAP 2019 guidelines. For children under 5, parent management training and behaviour therapy are tried before medication. Psychoeducation is recommended at every stage.

faqs.question: Can ADHD be treated without medication?
faqs.answer: Behavioural therapy, organisational skills training, CBT, and school or workplace accommodations can meaningfully reduce ADHD impairment, particularly in mild cases or when medication is declined. However, for moderate to severe ADHD, medication combined with behavioural strategies consistently produces larger symptom reduction and functional gains than non-drug approaches alone.

faqs.question: How long do ADHD medications take to work?
faqs.answer: Stimulant medications typically produce noticeable symptom improvement within the first 1-2 weeks at the right dose. Full dose optimisation through titration takes 4-8 weeks. Non-stimulants like atomoxetine require 6-8 weeks to reach full therapeutic effect. Behavioural therapy benefits accumulate over 3-6 months.

faqs.question: Does ADHD go away with age?
faqs.answer: Overt hyperactivity typically diminishes through adolescence, but inattention and executive dysfunction persist in 50-65% of children into adulthood. Many adults require ongoing treatment. The presentation shifts rather than resolves: restlessness becomes internal, and organisational and emotional difficulties dominate.

faqs.question: Are ADHD medications safe for children?
faqs.answer: Stimulant medications have been studied in children for over 60 years and are considered safe under specialist supervision. Common side effects include appetite suppression, mild growth deceleration, and sleep difficulties. Serious cardiovascular events are rare and primarily concern children with pre-existing heart conditions screened at baseline.

faqs.question: Can girls and women have ADHD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Girls are diagnosed 2-3× less often than boys in childhood but have comparable rates in adulthood. Girls more often present with the inattentive type, which is less disruptive and therefore under-referred. Hormonal fluctuations at puberty, perimenopause, and during the menstrual cycle can worsen ADHD symptoms in women.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ADHD and ADD?
faqs.answer: ADD was an older diagnostic term for the predominantly inattentive presentation without significant hyperactivity. DSM-5 (2013) replaced ADD and ADHD as separate diagnoses with a single diagnosis, ADHD, subdivided into three presentations. ADD is now called ADHD — predominantly inattentive presentation.

faqs.question: Is ADHD genetic?
faqs.answer: ADHD has a heritability of approximately 74-76% based on large twin studies. A parent with ADHD confers a 40-57% risk in each child. Multiple common genetic variants, and some rare copy-number variants, are implicated. Genetic risk is the strongest single predictor of ADHD, though environmental factors modulate expression.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause ADHD-like symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anxiety commonly causes poor concentration, restlessness, and distractibility that mimic ADHD. The key distinction is that anxiety-driven inattention is dominated by worry and fear, whereas ADHD inattention reflects executive dysregulation regardless of mood state. Both conditions can and do co-occur in the same person.

faqs.question: What school accommodations help children with ADHD?
faqs.answer: Evidence-based accommodations include extended time on tests, preferential seating near the teacher, reduced-distraction test environments, chunked assignments with interim deadlines, movement breaks, and written instructions. In the UK these are documented in an Education, Health and Care (EHC) plan; in the US under a 504 plan or IEP.

faqs.question: Can ADHD cause depression?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 40-50% of adults with ADHD develop clinically significant depression at some point. Repeated academic, occupational, and social failures related to unmanaged ADHD symptoms drive demoralisation and secondary mood disorder. Emotional dysregulation, a core feature of ADHD, further increases vulnerability to depressive episodes.

faqs.question: What is the ADHD combined type?
faqs.answer: ADHD combined type (ADHD-C) is the most common presentation, meeting criteria for at least six inattentive symptoms and at least six hyperactive-impulsive symptoms. It causes the broadest functional impairment across school, work, and relationships and is typically associated with more pronounced executive dysfunction than either pure subtype.

regionalNames.name: Trastorno por déficit de atención e hiperactividad (TDAH)

metaDescription: ADHD: a neurodevelopmental disorder of inattention, hyperactivity and impulsivity affecting 5-7% of children. Symptoms, diagnosis and treatment reviewed.
